did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) via transfer learning with models such as VGG16, MobileNet, InceptionV3, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, Xception, InceptionRNV2, EfficientNetV2L, and NASNetLarge, to classify keloid images and differentiate them from benign and malignant skin disorders (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma) using non-dermatoscopic clinical images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and evaluation of nine Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models (VGG16, MobileNet, InceptionV3, etc.) on two specific datasets of non-dermatoscopic images to classify keloid versus benign/malignant skin disorders. It reports new results, including specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC-ROC) and comparisons with previous studies, rather than summarizing existing literature.

title: Deep Learning Approaches for the Classification of Keloid Images in the Context of Malignant and Benign Skin Disorders
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of misdiagnosing keloids by classifying them among benign and malignant skin disorders using non-dermatoscopic clinical images. The researchers combined two public datasets containing images of melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, nevus, and keloids. They employed transfer learning with nine Convolutional Neural Network models, including VGG16, MobileNet, and DenseNet121, to perform binary and multiclass classification tasks. The VGG16 model achieved the highest overall performance, yielding an accuracy of 0.985 and an AUC-ROC of 0.996 for keloid classification. Additionally, DenseNet121 demonstrated superior performance in differentiating keloids from other malignant disorders with similar clinical presentations. The findings highlight the potential of deep learning for diagnosing keloids using accessible smartphone camera images where dermatoscopes are unavailable.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary focus of the study; classified as a benign skin disorder in the first task and as a distinct class in the second and third tasks. Derived from a dataset of 274 non-dermatoscopic images [44].

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant; malignant vs. benign vs. keloid; keloid vs. basal cell carcinoma vs. squamous cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs three distinct classification tasks: 1) Binary classification of skin lesions as malignant or benign; 2) Multiclass classification of skin disorders as malignant, benign, or keloid; 3) Multiclass classification to differentiate keloids from similar-looking malignant lesions (basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma). The models predict discrete class labels using CNNs with transfer learning.

datasets_used.name: PAD Dataset (Publicly Available Clinical Skin Lesion Dataset)
datasets_used.description: A dataset of non-dermatoscopic clinical images obtained using a smartphone-based application to help doctors and medical students collect clinical photos of skin lesions. It contains images taken from 1373 patients between 2018 and 2019. The dataset includes clinical diagnosis, patient age, lesion location, and other attributes. 58.4% of skin lesions and 100% of skin cancers are histopathology-confirmed.

use.training: Combined with the keloid dataset, split into 80% training and 20% testing. The training portion was further split into 90% for model training and 10% for validation. Random oversampling and data augmentation (flip, rotation, zoom, shift, shear) were applied only to the training set.
use.validation: 10% of the training split was used for validation with original (non-augmented) images.
use.testing: 20% of the combined dataset was used for testing with original (non-augmented) images.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Keloid Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of non-dermatoscopic keloid images sourced from Kaggle (referenced as [44] in the paper). The authors selected 274 images and cropped them to ensure consistency with the zoomed-in images in the first dataset.

use.training: Combined with the PAD dataset. Split using the same stratified shuffle split method as the first dataset (80% train, 20% test). The training portion was further split into 90% for model training and 10% for validation. Random oversampling and data augmentation were applied only to the training set.
use.validation: 10% of the training split was used for validation with original (non-augmented) images.
use.testing: 20% of the combined dataset was used for testing with original (non-augmented) images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC, and comparisons were made based on these metric values without statistical significance testing.

architecture_used.main_model: Nine Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models using transfer learning: VGG16, MobileNet, InceptionV3, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, Xception, InceptionResNetV2, EfficientNetV2L, and NASNetLarge.
architecture_used.input_size: 128 x 128 x 3 (RGB format; original images were RGBA and converted to RGB).

base_models: VGG16: 13 convolutional layers (3x3 filters, stride 1, same padding), 5 max-pooling layers (2x2 stride 2), 3 dense layers. ReLU activation for conv/dense layers, softmax for output.
DenseNet121: Feedforward network with densely connected convolutional layers within dense blocks and transition layers. 121 layers total.
InceptionV3: Uses Inception modules to minimize parameters by concatenating convolutional filters of various sizes.
MobileNet: Uses depthwise separable convolutions (depthwise convolution + 1x1 pointwise convolution) to reduce computational cost.
EfficientNetB0: Based on mobile inverted bottleneck convolution (MBConv) blocks. Scales depth, width, and resolution using a compound coefficient.
InceptionResNetV2: Second version of InceptionResNet model.
EfficientNetV2L: Largest variant of EfficientNetV2, uses fused-MBConv blocks in early layers and MBConv in later layers, optimized with neural architecture search (NAS).
architecture_details.transfer_learning_modification: Top classification layers (outputting 1000 classes) were removed. Replaced with a custom hidden classification block consisting of: GlobalAveragePooling2D layer, a dense layer with 1024 units and ReLU activation, a dropout layer with rate 0.5, and a final dense output layer (1 unit for binary, 3 units for multiclass) with sigmoid (binary) or softmax (multiclass) activation.
architecture_details.fine_tuning: Two cases were explored: 1) Freezing base model weights (training only added layers). 2) Fine-tuning base model (unfreezing all layers of the base model alongside the added model).

training_details.learning_rate: 0.0001 for training without fine-tuning; 0.00001 for training with fine-tuning.
training_details.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy for binary classification; Categorical cross-entropy for multiclass classification.
training_details.data_augmentation: Random oversampling on training set; image flip, rotation, zoom, shift, and shear applied to random sample of each class in original training dataset.
training_details.preprocessing: Pixel values rescaled to 0-1; images resized to 128x128.

classification_or_output_head.structure: GlobalAveragePooling2D -> Dense(1024, ReLU) -> Dropout(0.5) -> Dense(units, activation)
classification_or_output_head.units: 1 unit for binary classification (sigmoid activation); 3 units for multiclass classification (softmax activation).
classification_or_output_head.activation_functions: ReLU for hidden dense layer; Sigmoid for binary output; Softmax for multiclass output.

preprocessing_techniques: Pixel value rescaling to range between 0 and 1
Conversion of RGBA images to RGB format
Cropping of keloid images to ensure consistency with zoomed-in images in the first dataset
Stratified shuffle split for dataset partitioning to preserve class proportions
Random oversampling on the training set to address class imbalance
Data augmentation on the training set including image flip, rotation, zoom, shift, and shear

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set to tackle class imbalance and increase dataset size. Only applied to training sets; validation and test sets used original images.

conclusions: Deep learning algorithms, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using transfer learning, show potential for identifying and classifying benign skin disorders like keloids, which are often overlooked in favor of cancer-focused studies due to data scarcity.
The VGG16 model (after fine-tuning) achieved the best overall performance for classifying keloid images among benign and malignant skin lesions, yielding an accuracy of 0.985 and an AUC-ROC of 0.996.
The DenseNet121 model demonstrated superior performance specifically in differentiating keloids from other malignant disorders that have similar clinical presentations.

The study highlights limitations related to the collection and processing of non-dermatoscopic clinical images, such as issues arising from zooming in on distant images (leading to blurring) and the small size of training datasets which may not cover all lesion types or anatomical regions.

The findings suggest the utility of deep learning for diagnosing keloids using accessible smartphone camera images in settings where dermatoscopes are unavailable, while acknowledging the need to address data availability and image processing challenges.
important_remarks: The study relies on two small, publicly available datasets (PAD dataset with 2298 images and a Kaggle keloid dataset with 274 images), which limits generalizability and may not cover all lesion types or anatomical regions.
The dataset is imbalanced, requiring random oversampling and data augmentation (flip, rotation, zoom, shift, shear) on the training set to address class imbalance.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; model comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC-ROC).
The models were trained and tested on non-dermatoscopic clinical images to simulate settings where dermatoscopes are unavailable, but this introduces variability in image quality and zoom levels.
Testing on three new anonymized clinical images revealed issues with image processing: zooming in on distant images led to blurring, which negatively impacted classification performance.
The training data may not include all possible anatomical regions (e.g., near the eye), potentially limiting the model's ability to generalize to lesions in under-represented areas.
The study highlights that keloids are often overlooked in favor of cancer-focused studies due to data scarcity, and this work addresses that gap by focusing on benign keloid classification.
VGG16 achieved the highest overall performance (accuracy 0.985, AUC-ROC 0.996) for keloid classification, while DenseNet121 performed best in differentiating keloids from similar malignant lesions.
The paper acknowledges that deep learning algorithms have drawbacks, including dependency on image quality and the need for larger, more diverse datasets for robust clinical validation.
